text: that would enable us to explain and predict when and where, and under what exact conditions, revolutions have occurred and will occur. As argued by Turner, events Liminal Politics: Towards an Anthropology of Political Revolutions 213 and performances are not simply structured, but have their own life. We have to study such moments as real instances of contingency, moments where meaning- formation and symbolism condense and take new forms. That is far from saying that there are no recognizable patterns in political revolutions. In fact, the van Gennep-Turner framework proposed here does indicate shared patterns as well as shared dangers and problematics playing out in revolutionary processes. We would like to support the revolutions in the Arab world as a step toward more democracy, freedom and justice. We tend to forget that they are directed against political leaderships that were or are themselves fashioned on mimetic experiments of Western revolutionary traditions, calling for freedom, brotherhood and justice. In their national forms that emerged in the postcolonial setting, they merely gave new ideological impetus and fervour to what was already an inherent feature of the modern world. It is not easy to make discernments and take positions in the current situation: power structures are complex and available models for political emancipation all seem overburdened. But what seems clear enough is that we need better ethnographic accounts of revolutions in their unfolding. Revolutions serve as zero points of history, dramatic moments of foundation for the most diverse political systems around the globe, including Iran, America, Russia, Egypt, France, China, England, Cuba and Libya. Both liberalism and socialism are founded on revolutionary appeals and display in their differing idioms what one might term a ‘revolutionary epistemology’. This indicates that we have to disentangle the study of revolutions from their connected ideologies and make comparisons at the level of ritualization and symbolism. It also indicates that we somehow still have to come to terms with the fact that our modern world is inherently built on a series of revolutions, liminal origins to which we problematically return. This represents a foundational aspect of political modernity that anthropological tools can help to throw a critical light on. And it is this liminal aspect of modernity that can now be fully explicated. This page has been left blank intentionally Chapter 9 By Way of Conclusion: Out of Liminality The ritual sequence, or rather, the processual, playful order that Arnold van Gennep detected and named in his study of cultural ceremonies, contains at the centre, in the middle of everything, a phase and space of liminality. This was not something that van Gennep invented or a theory he ‘constructed’. It was something he literally dis-covered; it was already there, and Marcel Mauss could have done better than to ridicule van Gennep’s ground-breaking work for ‘stating the obvious’. If there is one reason that we still read Mauss’ work on gift-giving today, then it is not only for its honest and engaged use of the existing ethnographic record for a comparative purpose: it is precisely this ‘A-ha!’ sensation that it brings to the reader in an almost timeless way. It says something so simple and evident that we simply have to give in and realize that giving, receiving and giving back lies at the heart of meaningful sociability. Everywhere. Arnold van Gennep’s dis-covery of the tripartite structure of rituals became the ordering device of his immense life’s work. It was an illumination for himself. After his death, and with the English publication of Rites of Passage, it also became his belated claim to fame. Most people who know the name Arnold van Gennep know him for that one idea. It brought attention to the highly dynamic and transformative aspects of ritual which were wholly absent from the framework of Durkheim. This is one main reason for which Arnold van Gennep must be repositioned, and without any ‘but’, as a founding figure in the study not only of religion, but also of social life and social change in the most general way, far more useful than the conceptual and methodological apparatus set up by Durkheim. Edmund Leach and his British colleagues discovered this half a century ago and were quite explicit about it. Contrary to what one might have expected, this did not lead to any thorough revisiting of the life and work of Arnold van Gennep. Sociologists still today do not consider him. This might still change, especially now that we are no longer under the spell of any of those -isms that paradigmatically came to dominate the sciences throughout the twentieth century. Arnold van Gennep is the thinker of liminality. His own life was much stamped by critical threshold experiences, and also by his failed rite of passage into institutionalized academia. Van Gennep is without doubt the most published social scientist in France who failed to secure a job in a university. He eventually managed to throw off his bitterness, walk through another door, and give himself a new frame of work and purpose in life through his almost heroic, single-handed building up of French folklore. Van Gennep introduced the concept of liminality, and he analysed it with reference to a vast array of cultural rituals and practices. However, he was acutely Liminality and the Modern 216 aware that rituals, precisely in order to display an order, require closure and an act of reintegration. Liminality has its place within a sequence. No culture would be foolish enough to devise a ritual sequence that stops at the liminal. Without a return to normality and background structures that one can take for granted (at least until they are shaken again), individuals go crazy and societies become pathological. Human life ceases to be meaningful in perpetual liminality. And yet, there is an extent to which this is exactly what happened in one specific transition, the one that lies at the heart of social theorizing: the transition to modernity.
